kept.text:
A 25 year old just turned $225 million into $5.5 billion in 12 months.

Here’s exactly what he bought.

Leopold Aschenbrenner got fired from OpenAI in April 2024.

He spent the next few months writing a 165-page thesis predicting AGI by 2027.

Then he launched a fund and put his money where his thesis was.

He bought zero Nvidia. Zero Microsoft. Zero Google. Zero Amazon.

He bought what AI actually runs on.

Bloom Energy (BE), power infrastructure for data centers. Up 1,422% in one year.

Lumentum (LITE), optical components that move data between chips. Up 1,331%.

Sandisk (SNDK), storage. Up 3,130%.

CoreWeave (CRWV), GPU cloud infrastructure. Up 166%.

Iris Energy (IREN), AI computing and data centers. Up 583%.

The thesis was simple: every AI company needs energy, bandwidth, storage, and compute.

Nobody was buying those. Everyone was buying the AI companies themselves.

He was right.

His fund now manages $6 billion. Backed by Patrick and John Collison of Stripe and former GitHub CEO Nat Friedman.

I’m adding this to my watchlist.

Every time he files a new 13F, we will break it down here.

Turn on notifications so you don’t miss the alert, this is VERY important.

Many people will wish they followed us sooner.

profile_bio.description: Private educational community for serious long-term investors. NEXT OPENING: MAY 18

kept.text:
"You have all this money. What should Nvidia be doing with it?" Dwarkesh asked Jensen.

He's paying CoreWeave $6.3 billion to keep reselling Nvidia's chips to OpenAI and Meta.

The math on cutting CoreWeave out is real. They bought Nvidia chips, packaged them as cloud compute, and signed $55.6 billion in customer contracts. OpenAI committed up to $22.4 billion. Meta $14.2 billion. Microsoft $10 billion. Every dollar runs on Nvidia silicon. Nvidia's own gross margin already runs 75.2%. CoreWeave clears similar margins on the rentals. The capacity to vertically integrate is sitting right there.

Instead Jensen signed a take-or-pay backstop on CoreWeave's unsold capacity through April 2032. If the racks sit idle, Nvidia pays. He is paying his reseller to stay between him and the customer.

His answer was a recipe. "As much as needed, but as little as possible." The work Nvidia must do is the work nobody else will do. 20 years of CUDA. The architecture. The tooling. Everything else, push to the ecosystem.

Now look at what staying out of the cloud margin actually buys him. CoreWeave carries the capex and the multi-decade real estate leases. CoreWeave eats every data center delivery delay. Nvidia recognizes GPU revenue when chips ship and never holds an empty rack on its balance sheet. The $6.3 billion never appears as a long-term cloud commitment. It is structured as an option Nvidia would happily exercise.

Then there is the line Jensen was most direct about. He told Dwarkesh that Nvidia would be "at the top of the list not to make it" if it had stuck rigidly to first instincts. Nvidia's original graphics architecture was precisely wrong. The company reasoned its way to CUDA from first principles. Jensen has the humility to know the next architecture call could be wrong too.

The day Nvidia operates the cloud is the day every wrong architecture bet becomes stranded compute on its own books.

A $5 trillion company sells the foundation, takes the architecture risk, and pays the ecosystem to handle everything else.

The backstop is what Jensen pays to never become the cloud.

tweet.text:
$IREN, $NBIS, $WULF, $CIFR, $CRWV

One thing that stood out to me after going through the latest earnings from Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet is that the constraint is "time to compute".

All three are effectively saying the same thing, just in different ways. They need GPU compute capacity now, and they cannot build it fast enough internally.

> The important distinction here is that they have no issue increasing capex to meet demand.

Demand is operating on a near-term, almost real-time basis, while supply is constrained by the physical realities of infrastructure buildout.

> Scaling AI infrastructure takes time. Power needs to be secured, land needs to be developed, permits need to be approved, and data centers need to be built and fitted with hardware that is itself supply constrained.

When hyperscalers cannot meet demand internally, they are forced to look externally, and that naturally shifts the value toward whoever can actually deliver compute on the timelines that matter. Right now, that incremental spend is increasingly flowing to neoclouds.

profile_bio.description: Independent Analyst. Writing about Finance. Not Financial Advice. DYOR. Subscribe for deeper insights into my portfolio.

tweet.text:
This week’s earnings reports made the infrastructure story hard to ignore.

Every major cloud player is committing billions in capex — not on models, but on the physical layer running them. 

GPUs, data centers, power contracts.
The companies supplying that capacity - $CRWV, $NBIS, $CIFR, $IREN - are called neoclouds. They rent compute to labs & enterprises who can’t build their own infrastructure.

Demand for compute hours is outpacing supply. Labs are on waitlists. Deals are being signed years out.

The bottleneck in AI isn’t intelligence. It’s infrastructure.

tweet.text:
$GLXY

So, the first data hall was delivered to $CRWV and Helios is now operational.

Interestingly, the stock has done well post-earnings despite a run up prior. This is a different reaction to what we’ve seen in previous quarters where even strong results were often met with skepticism or indifference.

I think a big part of that comes down to tone.

Management sounded far more serious, direct, and measured when discussing Helios. 

Over the past few quarters, many critics, including some $GLXY holders, have questioned Novo and the team for what they saw as a lack of urgency or decisiveness around Helios.

Personally, I do not see that as a major issue.

If anything, long-term shareholders should take some comfort from the fact that management has been focused on execution rather than constantly trying to talk up the asset. In AI infrastructure, there is a fine line between creating excitement and overpromising. 

$GLXY has chosen the quieter route, and while that has clearly frustrated the market at times, the first delivery to CoreWeave is tangible proof that this is no longer just a story.

This is not to say there aren’t risks. 

$GLXY has clearly underperformed many AI infrastructure peers over the past year, and that has created real discomfort among shareholders. The concern, which I share, is that the stock could miss the broader infrastructure re-rating if the market continues to reward more obvious AI names while ignoring $GLXY’s progress.

In other words, the risk is not just execution risk but perception risk.

The market may eventually acknowledge Helios, but timing matters. If the AI infrastructure wave peaks before $GLXY is properly recognised as a serious beneficiary, then shareholders may not get the full upside they hoped for.

profile_bio.description: I write about asymmetric investments that go unnoticed, with a deep focus on emerging markets.

quoted_tweet.text:
$GLXY

A large reason for the market completely overlooking its datacenter business, Helios, is a result of its lack of confidence that $CRWV will fulfil the 15-year, $1B annual contract for the first 800MW.

At current prices, $GLXY's Helios is priced at zero.

The stock currently trades at $8.45B. Net Digital Assets + Cash & Stablecoins make up half of that at $4.3B. The Digital Assets operating business brought in $505M in Adj. Gross Profit in FY25. If we assume just an 8x P/GP multiple here, it would imply a $4B market cap.

At these very conservative estimates, the data center business is valued at $0.

In essence, my thesis for $GLXY in terms of valuation upside, is the value of Helios. So how much can Helios really be worth?

At a 90% EBITDA margin on a $1B average annual contract, and a 20x EV/EBITDA multiple (avg data center REIT trades at 26x), we get $18B in enterprise value. Subtracting construction financing, we arrive at $15B in equity value.

That implies nearly 200% upside from the first 800MW that is already approved and contracted. 

Last week, we had very favourable news for $CRWV. It signed a new deal with Anthropic and an expanded deal with $META. $CRWV services 9 out of the top 10 AI providers in the world. The complete lack of confidence by the market in its ability to fulfil the $GLXY contract is where the upside lies.

That’s not all. $GLXY has another 830MW approved by ERCOT that will almost certainly be contracted to a hyper-scaler. 

In my view, that will be the turning point for a quick re-rating of the stock to finally acknowledge $GLXY as a data center player as much as it is a crypto infrastructure business. The market can ignore a singular 800MW tranche contracted to $CRWV but it cannot ignore a second 800MW tranche contracted to a hyper-scaler.
